coach: What exactly are you deciding? What are the stakes, and how reversible is it? Is there a deadline? What's your current lean right now — and what is it resting on?
human:
I'm deciding whether to relocate my family from Atlanta to San Francisco for a job offer. I'd be the VP of Engineering at a Series B startup — it's a big step up, but it means uprooting my wife and two kids, selling our house, and leaving behind a really solid life we've built here.

The stakes are huge. It's about our entire lifestyle, our kids' schools and friendships, my wife's career, our financial stability. It's not easily reversible — if we go and it fails, we'd be coming back with nothing, a burned bridge at my current company, and a resentful family.

Deadline is two weeks. The CEO wants an answer by then.

My lean right now is to take it. I'm 90% there. The title, the equity upside, the chance to build something from the ground up — it's what I've been working toward my whole career. I've been a director at a big company for six years, and I'm tired of the politics. This feels like my shot. My gut says go.

coach: Let me push on the framing. What if the real question isn't the one you just posed, but something underneath it? Whose interest does your current framing serve? And what are you assuming that, if it turned out false, would flip this decision?
human:
That's... a sharp way to put it. Let me sit with that.

Whose interest does my framing serve? Honestly? Mine. The version of me that wants the corner office, the board seat, the story to tell at reunions. I'm framing it as "my big career move" — but I'm the one who'd be commuting, flying back some weekends, while my wife handles the school drop-offs and the soccer practices alone. She's been clear she's willing but not excited. I've been treating that as "support" when maybe it's "sacrifice."

What I'm assuming that could flip this... I'm assuming the equity will be worth something. I'm assuming the company's trajectory is real, not just founder charisma. I'm assuming my family will adapt within a year. And I'm assuming my current company won't counteroffer or that I don't care if they do.

If any of those turned out false — especially the first two — I'd be looking at a very expensive mistake with no safety net.

coach: What's the reference class here — when people in your position make this kind of call, how does it usually turn out? Does your lean survive that base rate honestly?
human:
You know, that's uncomfortable to think about.

The reference class would be... senior eng leaders at big companies who jump to a Series B VP role. I've seen maybe a dozen cases personally, and another dozen through my network. Honestly? Maybe 4 out of those 24 worked out well — equity paid off, family adjusted, career advanced. The rest either flamed out within 18 months, had the company pivot or die, or the family strain was too much and they moved back.

So base rate is something like 15-20% success. That's brutal to admit.

My lean doesn't survive that honestly. I've been telling myself I'm special — that this CEO is different, that this product is different, that my family is tougher. But the numbers say I'm probably not. The rational part of me knows that. The emotional part just... doesn't want to hear it.

coach: Now imagine it's a year from now and this decision clearly went badly. You're looking back at the wreckage. What was the cause?
human:
I can see it pretty clearly.

The cause was that I underestimated how much my wife would struggle being isolated out there. She left a job she loved, a network of close friends, her parents who help with the kids. In San Francisco, she's alone in a new city with no support system while I'm working 70-hour weeks trying to prove myself. The resentment builds. We start fighting. The kids feel it.

Meanwhile, the company misses its growth targets. The Series C falls through. My equity is worthless. The CEO starts blaming the leadership team. I'm on a PIP within 18 months.

And the worst part? I can't even call my old boss and ask for my job back, because I was so smug about leaving. I burned that bridge with a dramatic exit speech about how I was "ready for real impact."

So I'm stuck — no job, no savings buffer from the move, a damaged marriage, and kids who hate me for taking them away from their friends. The cause was hubris. I thought I was the exception to the rule.

coach: Which parts of this are you genuinely sure about, and which are you guessing? And be honest with me — what do you know about the specifics here (the people, the money, the timing, the fine print) that you haven't told me yet? What would a close friend say you're avoiding?
human:
You're asking me to dig into the parts I've been glossing over. Okay.

What I'm genuinely sure about: I'm qualified for the role technically. I can manage a team, I know the space, I've built similar products before. I'm also sure my wife loves me and wants me to be happy. And I'm sure the company has real revenue — about $3M ARR, growing 15% month over month. That part checks out.

What I'm guessing: I'm guessing the CEO is someone I can work with long-term. We've had four conversations, all great, but I've never seen him under real pressure. I'm guessing the board will support the next round. I'm guessing my kids will be fine after six months. I'm guessing my wife's career will recover.

Here's what I haven't told you — and a close friend would call me out on this: the equity package is structured as incentive stock options with a 4-year vest and a 1-year cliff. But they're priced at the last round's valuation, which was already marked up 40% in the secondary market. So even if the company does okay, I might never see a dollar. I've done the math. I'd need at least a $200M exit to make back what I'm giving up in total comp from my current job.

And the thing I'm really avoiding? My dad took a big swing like this when I was 12. It failed. We moved back to a smaller house. He never recovered professionally. I've been telling myself that's irrelevant — that I'm not him — but I'm starting to wonder if this whole chase is me trying to rewrite his story.

coach: Is there anything in the details that actually cuts against what you want to do — some fact that, if you're honest with yourself, points the other way?
human:
Yeah. There is.

One thing I haven't mentioned: the current VP of Engineering is leaving. That's the role I'd fill. I asked why, and the CEO said "cultural fit" — vague, diplomatic. I pressed once, got a non-answer. I didn't press again because I didn't want to hear it. A close friend who knows the company's investors told me off the record that the last VP clashed hard with the CEO over roadmap priorities and was pushed out. That's a pattern, and I'm ignoring it.

Another thing: my wife has a job offer here in Atlanta that's a real step up for her — partner track at a boutique law firm. She hasn't said it outright, but if we move, she's walking away from that. She told me last night, quietly, that she's "nervous about starting over." I heard her, but I changed the subject to school districts in Palo Alto.

And the one that really stings: I checked the cap table. The CEO and the seed investors own 80% combined. The option pool is thin. I'd be taking all the risk for a slice of something I don't control. That's not a partnership. That's a bet on someone else's horse.

So yes. There are facts that cut hard against my lean. I've been holding them at arm's length.
